steps.text: Read the original dataset from the histogram. The 14 salmon fall in these weight ranges (using midpoints): positions 1–3 at ~12.5 lb, positions 4–9 at ~17.5 lb, positions 10–11 at ~22.5 lb, position 12 at ~27.5 lb, positions 13–14 at ~32.5 lb. The original mean is \(\frac{3(12.5)+6(17.5)+2(22.5)+1(27.5)+2(32.5)}{14} = \frac{280}{14} = 20\) lb.

Analyze the median. With 14 values, the median is the average of the 7th and 8th ordered values. Both the 7th and 8th values fall in the 17.5 lb group, so the original median \(= 17.5\) lb. Adding 68 lb creates a 15-value dataset; the new median is the 8th value. Since 68 lb is added at the high end, the 8th position is unchanged at 17.5 lb. The median does NOT increase.

Analyze the mean. The new value is 68 lb. Because \(68 > 20\) (the original mean), adding it must increase the mean: \(\text{new mean} = \frac{280+68}{15} = \frac{348}{15} = 23.2\) lb. The mean DOES increase.

Evaluate each choice:
  • A: I only — the median does not increase; this is wrong. Eliminated.
  • B: II only — the mean increases (from 20 to 23.2 lb) but the median stays at 17.5 lb. That matches choice B — B is correct.
  • C: I and II — the median does not increase, so "I and II" is false. Eliminated.
  • D: Neither I nor II — the mean does increase, so "neither" is false. Eliminated.
